narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows a commitment to pursuing law, but the admissions model focused on your potential to pass the bar exam on the first try has flagged some concerns. The model predicts a 43.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application—law schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and avoid high attrition risks. Among all the factors in your background, three stood out as having the strongest influence on this prediction: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the typical 5.53 average), and your LSAT score of 25.50 (significantly lower than the average of 36.70). Your undergraduate GPA was the most powerful positive factor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by demonstrating stronger academic performance than many peers might show. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking had a notable negative pull, signaling to the model that your performance relative to classmates slipped in later years, increasing the perceived risk of bar failure. Your LSAT score also contributed toward the failure prediction, as its below-average level raised doubts about your readiness for the rigors ahead, even though your middle family income bracket and full-time status aligned with the norm for most applicants. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where strong LSATs and consistent decile rankings often predict success, your combination here created a riskier profile despite your decent GPA holding things back from an even worse outlook. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your effort—it's the model weighing patterns from thousands of past students to forecast bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted probability dramatically in your favor. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these exact areas, and with your full-time dedication, you're well-positioned to do the same. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that others lack.
